coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots structural characterization by solution-state NMR and UV–visible spectroscopy and DFT modeling
coredata.dc:creator: Arroyave, J.M.
Ambrusi, R.E.
Robein, Y.
Pronsato, M.E.
Brizuela, G.
Di Nezio, M.S.
Centurión, M.E.
coredata.dc:description: A combination of experimental and theoretical results was used for the structural characterization of Carbon dots (Cdots). The synthesis of the Cdots was performed using glucose as a precursor and via the microwave method (“bottom-up” route) as a simple and environmentally friendly method. The experimental data obtained by UV–visible, fluorescence, IR and solution-state NMR spectroscopy techniques were correlated with density functional theory calculations results. Particularly, the results obtained by solution-state NMR spectroscopy suggested Cdots structures of Polymer dots (PDs) and/or polymer Carbon dots (PCDs) kind. PDs are characterized by being formed only of polymer structures and PCDs by being formed of a small carbonized core surrounded by polymer structures. Calculations allow us to predict the UV–visible absorption and IR spectra behavior of the species detected by the NMR technique, and propose a structure that can explain the experimentally observed features and infer the presence or absence of a carbon core in the proposed structures. Density of states curves were used to analyze and interpret the theoretical spectra peaks. Additionally, a calculation of the IR spectra for the models proposed, was performed. The complement of experimental and theoretical results represents a useful tool for the structural characterization of Cdots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Kind of Carbon dots
Glucose
Characterization
solution-state NMR
UV–visible
IR
DFT
TDDFT

Agencia Nacional de Promoción Científica y Tecnológica

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the financial support from SGCyT-UNS (PGI–UNS 24/Q097 and PGI-UNS 24/F072) and ANPCyT (PICT-2016-4094). Ambrusi, R. E., Robein, Y., Pronsato, M. E., and Brizuela, G. are members of CONICET and Arroyave, J. M. is a Postdoctoral fellow of this institution. We also thank the University of Trieste (Italy) for its kind hospitality during the NMR data collection.

We acknowledge the financial support from SGCyT-UNS (PGI?UNS 24/Q097 and PGI-UNS 24/F072) and ANPCyT (PICT-2016-4094). Ambrusi, R. E. Robein, Y. Pronsato, M. E. and Brizuela, G. are members of CONICET and Arroyave, J. M. is a Postdoctoral fellow of this institution. We also thank the University of Trieste (Italy) for its kind hospitality during the NMR data collection.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: . a. TEM image and b. Frequency histogram vs. size of Cdots[3,6].
. a. UV–visible and b. PL of the fractions during the SEC purification of Cdots.
. 1H NMR spectra of Cdots a. after, b. before dialysis and c. glucose.
. Proposed monomers on the polymeric structure of the Cdots. a Butanoic Acid and b. An unidentified aldehyde.
. a. Diagram to describe the reaction process to prepare PDs and PCDs by the “bottom-up” route. b. Polymer carbon dots (PCDs) proposed in this work.
. UV–visible spectra at B3LYP level; a. Coronene, b. Anthracene.
. TDOS and PDOS at B3LYP level; a. Coronene, b. Anthracene.
. IR vibrational spectra at PBE level; a. Butanoic acid and Butanal, b. Coronene and Anthracene.
. 1H NMR, 13C NMR, CHSQC and gCOSY-NMR signals from Cdots.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots structural characterization by solution-state NMR and UV–visible spectroscopy and DFT modeling

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: INQUISUR, Department of Chemistry, Universidad Nacional del Sur, Av. Alem 1253, B8000CPB Bahía Blanca, Argentina
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: INQUISUR, Department of Chemistry, Universidad Nacional del Sur, Av. Alem 1253, B8000CPB Bahía Blanca, Argentina

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: IFISUR, Department of Physics, Universidad Nacional del Sur, Av. Alem 1253, B8000CPB Bahía Blanca, Argentina
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: IFISUR, Department of Physics, Universidad Nacional del Sur, Av. Alem 1253, B8000CPB Bahía Blanca, Argentina

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A combination of experimental and theoretical results was used for the structural characterization of Carbon dots (Cdots). The synthesis of the Cdots was performed using glucose as a precursor and via the microwave method (“bottom-up” route) as a simple and environmentally friendly method. The experimental data obtained by UV–visible, fluorescence, IR and solution-state NMR spectroscopy techniques were correlated with density functional theory calculations results. Particularly, the results obtained by solution-state NMR spectroscopy suggested Cdots structures of Polymer dots (PDs) and/or polymer Carbon dots (PCDs) kind. PDs are characterized by being formed only of polymer structures and PCDs by being formed of a small carbonized core surrounded by polymer structures. Calculations allow us to predict the UV–visible absorption and IR spectra behavior of the species detected by the NMR technique, and propose a structure that can explain the experimentally observed features and infer the presence or absence of a carbon core in the proposed structures. Density of states curves were used to analyze and interpret the theoretical spectra peaks. Additionally, a calculation of the IR spectra for the models proposed, was performed. The complement of experimental and theoretical results represents a useful tool for the structural characterization of Cdots.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon is one of the most abundant and well-known elements. When carbon structures of nanometric size are prepared, their properties are different from bulk carbon. Fullerenes and carbon nanotubes represent two main types of carbon-based materials [1]. In 2004 carbon dots (Cdots) were discovered, these are quasi-dimensional nanomaterials, the three dimensions are all 10 nm or less in size, so their shape is spherical[2]. This type of carbon material in particular contains different types of oxygen and hydrogen groups on their surface, providing excellent properties and motivating extensive of research[3–5]. Their main characteristics are high solubility in water, excellent biocompatibility, excellent optical properties, chemical inertness, despicable cytotoxicity and simple functionalization[6–11]. A further advantage of the Cdots is their easy, economical and environmentally friendly synthesis, which is performed using various techniques, resulting in a fluorescent nanomaterial with a very wide variety of applications[10,12].

In recent years Cdots have been classified and named in different groups; however, it has always been a difficult problem to reasonably classify and evaluate a new class of materials, especially for those with diverse structures and properties. Several definitions have been adopted by researchers to describe this kind of nanomaterial, such as carbon dots (Cdots)[3,9], carbon nanoparticles (CNPs)[13], carbon nanodots (CNDs)[14,15], graphene quantum dots (GQDs)[16,17], quantum dots of carbon (CQDs)[18,19] and polymer dots (PDs)[20,21]. Some definitions are too general to distinguish the different kinds of Cdots, and some even overlap and conflict. Despite the extensive debate on the classification and nomenclature of the Cdots[22], it became increasingly clear that the Cdots could be divided, due to their structural differences, in graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs, CQDs or Cdots) and polymer dots (PDs)[23]. Since 2015 a new material has been classified as carbon dots polymer (PCDs), which has a hybrid polymer/carbon structure in place of the main carbon shell structure. This material has hybrid structures and properties of PDs and Cdots due to incomplete carbonization after polymerization [23–25].

In general, synthetic approaches depend on the kind of Cdots and therefore on their structure and the desired properties[23,25]. Various source materials and synthetic approaches have been explored to manufacture different structures. In particular, surface groups such as amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups are the most common and these improve physicochemical properties, increasing their sensitivity and selectivity which subsequently helps for new applications[12,26].

Before characterizing and determining the appropriate application for the Cdots, it is important to separate and/or purify them after synthesis. Among the most commonly used methods are centrifugation, ultrasound, lyophilization, solvent washing, filtration and dialysis[27]. Chromatography is probably the most powerful technique for the separation and purification of organic compounds. However, very few studies have used column chromatography to purify Cdots solutions[28]. If the objective is to analyze the structure of the Cdots, as is the case of the study described here, the column chromatography is the most appropriate methodology for their purification and separation because it allows to obtain the fraction of Cdots free of other undesirable substances obtained during the synthesis process[28].

For the analysis of their several physicochemical properties, Cdots have been characterized by different techniques, such as X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), infrared (IR), SEM, TEM, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and particle size analysis[3,9,27]. The optical and morphological characteristics of Cdots attract great interest because they affect most of their properties. The optical characterization is normally performed by measuring UV–visible and their fluorescence, which is the most interesting and important property of this type of nanomaterial. Different characterization techniques exist for morphological studies, but microscopic techniques such as polarized optical microscopy (POM), SEM and TEM are the most important[1].

Determining the kind of Cdots and their structural characteristics is of fundamental relevance in the study of the composition and nature of these nanomaterials because they provide important information about their properties. XRD, energy dispersive X-ray (EDX), XPS, IR, Raman, BET, the Zeta size analyzer and NMR are the common techniques used to study structural properties[3,7–9,27]. For example, the vibrational characterization of nanoparticles is usually studied by FT-IR and Raman spectroscopy. These techniques are the most developed and practical in comparison with other elementary analytical methods. The most relevant function provided by these techniques is the determination of the Cdots fingerprint region[9].

The complete elucidation of chemical structure of Cdots is extremely important to understand how the chemical composition affects the optical properties and thus enable fine adjustment[11,23,25]. Analytical studies by NMR (nuclear magnetic resonance) spectroscopy can be used to identify the precise structure and chemical groups present on the surface and to determine if they are chemically altered during polymerization and carbonization. Examining the nature of their structure is useful to correlate their physicochemical properties. For example, studies have been carried out to identify and quantify fluorophore groups in the structure of Cdots, which are responsible for the fluorescence, the main characteristic of this kind of nanomaterials [29]. B. Zhi et. al. [30] performed more detailed experimental-theoretical studies on these fluorophore groups providing important hints on the origins and mechanism of the fluorescence of Cdots due to their structure. Recently, high-resolution magic angle NMR spectroscopy (HR-MAS) in solid-state has been proposed as an effective technique for the characterization of organic molecules bound to nanoparticles[31,32], although CP/MAS and MRI in high-resolution solution have also been successfully used[33]. However, these techniques are not economical and easily accessible. Therefore, more traditional solution-phase characterization techniques, such as proton and carbon NMR methods, could also be used for structural elucidation of Cdots. For example, a recent Cdots 13C NMR spectroscopic study suggested that these were of a graphitic structure containing carboxylic acid and carbonyl groups[34]. Many other NMR studies have been performed on this type of nanomaterials to explain their structure and/or characterize their surface modification[35]. To complement the information provided by the traditional NMR technique with theoretical calculations would facilitate the structural elucidation of the Cdots and a more detailed understanding of the kind of Cdots [36], contributing to a better interpretation of their optical properties and other related functions.

Electronic and optical properties were already studied theoretically for GQDs [37–42]. However, there is not yet a clear representation model to study PDs and PCDs from a theoretical point of view in order to understand and predict the main properties of this type of structures. Recently, a first attempt was performed to represent carbon Cdots surfaces [43]. But this approach could be further developed under the knowledge of new NMR results provided in the current publication.

In this work, we focus our attention on the structural characterization of Cdots. The kind of Cdots present and their possible structure will be determined by 1H and 13C solution-state NMR spectroscopy, exploiting the high solubility of Cdots in polar solvents. Due to amplification effects, it is assumed that solution-state NMR spectroscopy has limited usefulness for structure elucidation and solid-state HR-MAS techniques are considered more appropriate. Therefore, the main objective is to obtain data on the structure of the Cdots using solution-state NMR studies and to relate them to theoretical calculations to obtain more precise and reliable structures. To our knowledge, this is the first detailed study in which a detailed analysis for the structural assignment of the Cdots has been performed by solution-state NMR spectroscopy and theoretical calculations.

Cdots were synthesized by the “bottom-up” method, specifically with glucose as a precursor and by microwave treatment as described by Arroyave et al. [3]. Briefly, 3 mL of 0.31 g L-1 glucose marked with 13C (Sigma-Aldrich, Germany) at pH 10 was transferred to a 10 mL tube. Later, a conventional microwave treatment was performed at 350 W for 1 min. Milli Q water was used as a solvent. The characteristic brown color of the Cdots was observed.

Cdots obtained were filtered with a 0.1 μm pore size filter. Afterward, dialysis was performed with a membrane of 500 – 1000 Da. The membrane was activated with isopropyl alcohol and washed with Milli Q water. The Cdots solution was then introduced for 48 h and the water outside the membrane was replaced every 3 h to ensure no osmotic equilibrium and thus enable the output of the small molecules during the process[6,44].

Before the SEC, tests were performed to determine the optimum solvent by using thin-layer chromatography (TLC). Several solvent mixtures were tested; DMC:methanol (9:1), DMC:methanol:acetic acid (9:1:0.5), Ethanol, and Ethanol:acetic acid (9.5:0.5). TLC silica gel 260 F254 was used. After TLC, the sample was observed in a UV lamp. It was also revealed by the permanganate method. The best result was obtained with Ethanol as eluent. A low polar solvent like methanol is required, this was not used in TLC as it dissolves silica gel. Therefore, a Sephadex column was activated with methanol, and then the compound (after dialysis) was added. After 30 min, the fractions were collected. The solid passed through the column corresponds to 20 mL of sample. The fractions were controlled by UV–visible and fluorescence[45]. SEC, also called gel filtration chromatography, was used to guarantee the separation of any fragments that may have remained after the dialysis process. SEC is a size-based separation process. Particles with smaller or equal pore size in the packing materials permeate deeply into the column, while larger particles diffuse freely through the column [46].

The Cdots studied here were characterized by various techniques in previous works of our group (Zeta potential and Hydrodynamic size, TGA, DTA, and XRD)[3]. The electrical and optical properties were characterized by current–voltage curves[6]. Besides, The Cdots were characterized using theoretical[43] and experimental/theoretical studies[9].

Cdots UV–visible and molecular fluorescence spectra were obtained by UV–visible spectrophotometer (Varian Cary 5000) and a Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Varian Cary Eclipse equipped with a Varian Single Cell Peltier Accessory) respectively. As for fluorescence, the Cdots were diluted 30 times and excited at 350 nm[3,24].
Analyses were performed with a JEOL 100 CX II microscope, operated at 100 kV with magnification up to 100.000X[3,6].

20 mL of Cdots (≈ 6 mg) post-dialysis was evaporated at 40 °C. It is then dried at 40 °C under vacuum for 12 h. Once the water has been removed from the sample, the obtained solid was diluted in 0.6 mL deuterium oxide (99.96 atom % D. Aldrich Chemical Co., Inc.) and transferred into a 5 mm NMR tube. The studies were performed in 500 MHz equipment (Varian 500 MHz). High-resolution solution 1D (1H, 13C) and 2D (gCOSY, gHMQC, gHMBC) NMR spectra were recorded for Cdots. 1H–1H homonuclear gradient COSY 2D NMR spectra were obtained using 128 scans and 4 dummy scans with a recycle delay of 2.0 s. 1H–13C heteronuclear gradient multiple quantum correlation (gHMQC) and multiple bond correlation (gHMBC) 2D NMR spectra were obtained using 128 scans and 4 dummy scans with a recycle delay of 2.0 s. The data were analyzed with the “MestreNova” software[35,47].

IR spectra were acquired with a Nicolet iS50 470 spectrometer equipped with a deuterated triglycine sulfate (DTGS) detector, a SMART-ARK ATR accessory and a ZnSe crystal (area: 10 × 72 mm, incident angle: 45°, total refections: 12, through cell)[9].

The theoretical calculations were performed by Density functional theory (DFT) and time dependent functional theory (TDDFT) using the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP)[48,49], which employs a plane-wave basis set and a periodic supercell method. The calculations were performed at two levels of theory: employing the generalized gradient corrected approximation (GGA) functional of Perdew, Burke, and Ernzerhof (PBE)[50] and employing the B3LYP hybrid functional[51]. The projector-augmented-wave (PAW) method[52,53] was used to solve variatonally the Kohn–Sham equations with a plane-wave basis set expansion up to a cutoff energy of 400 eV. Gamma-centered Monkhorst–Pack scheme[54] with 1 × 1 × 1 k-points grid for the integration over the Brillouin zone was employed in calculations. The electronic states partial occupations near the Fermi level, were modeled within a Gaussian smearing approach with a 0.2 eV smearing width.

Geometry relaxations were performed by minimizing the total energy of the supercell using a conjugated gradient algorithm to relax ions[55], until it converged within 10−4 eV and the forces on each ion were less than 0.01 eV °A−1. These optimizations were performed at PBE level only, with the Grimme's DFT-D2 method to take into account van der Waals interactions (vdW)[56]. Then the electronic structure was obtained at B3LYP level. The UV–visible spectra, the density of states (DOS) and partial density of states (PDOS) were obtained using both functionals.

All Cdots model calculations were performed in a 30 × 30 × 30 Å box, with the aim to achieve the optimized isolated structure, as was performed in previous works following graphene quantum dots model[37,39].

Additionally VASP results were post processed using PHONOPY code[57] for frequency calculations with the harmonic approximation and the obtaining of the IR vibrational spectra following the routine proposed by Skelton et al. [58].

Fig. 1
(a) shows the size and shape of the Cdots that were examined by TEM. The nanoparticles obtained were spherical in shape with an average size of 8 nm. Fig. 1 (b) shows the size frequency histogram of the nanoparticles, most of which are between 6 and 8 nm.

After dialysis, chromatography studies were performed to obtain a more purified Cdots structure. Thin-layer chromatography presented the best elution results using methanol as solvent. Subsequently, SEC purification was performed and fractions were controlled by UV–visible and Fluorescence. Fig. 2 shows the UV–visible and PL spectra during the SEC of Cdots. Fig. 2 (a) shows the UV–visible bands, identifying two absorption bands at 270 and 338 nm characteristic for Cdots. The 270 and 338 nm bands correspond to C = C and COO– groups respectively[3,43]. Fig. 2 (b) shows PLs emission bands at 440 nm when excited at 350 nm which is characteristic for Cdots[3]. Fractions 6, 7 and 8 were combined; both the UV–visible and PL spectra of these fractions contain the most defined and representative bands of the Cdots[3] (see Fig. 6).

In order to identify the changes caused after purification, Figure S1 shows the comparison of the NMR spectra of Cdots before (post-dialysis) and after SEC. The only difference observed is between 2 and 3 ppm. In this range, H signals close to an oxygen atom are present (H-C-CO)[59]. These signals are not present after SEC probably due to the retention of unreacted fragments in the pores of the chromatographic column.

In addition, Fig. 3 shows the high-resolution 1H NMR spectra of Cdots (before and after dialysis) and glucose in order to corroborate the formation of Cdots. The signal at 4.6 ppm due to the presence of deuterium oxide used as solvent was suppressed. Both in the Cdots 1H NMR spectra before and after dialysis signals are observed at approximately 1.3 and 1.8–1.95 ppm possibly due to the presence of hydrogen (H) from non-cyclic alkanes[59]. Signals between 3.5 and 4.3 ppm may be due to the presence of oxygen-containing groups (alcohols, esters or carboxylic acids). Finally, the signal near 8.5 ppm is due to an aldehyde group[59]. The most notable difference between the Cdots NMR spectra before and after the dialysis process is observed between 3.5 and 4.3 ppm which is due to oxygen-containing groups. According to the NMR results, small molecules (smaller than the pore size of the dialysis membrane) containing oxygenated groups are present and they are probably from unreacted monomers.

It is also important to mention that the remaining glucose molecules that may have been present after synthesis was eliminated after dialysis. In both Cdots spectra of Fig. 3 (a) and (b), glucose is not present. The 5.25 ppm signal of glucose corresponding to the H (carboxyl neighbor) anomaly disappears by the aperture of the glucose ring[59]. 1H NMR, 13C NMR and gCOSY-NMR ( Figure S2 ) confirms the transformation of the glucose residues into Cdots with the presence of coating alkyl chains[60].

Once the formation of Cdots was confirmed and after their purification, studies of 13C NMR and 1H NMR were performed with the selected fractions (Fractions 6, 7 and 8 of SEC). Moreover, 2D studies that relate the 1H–1H and 13C–1H were performed, which provide detailed information on the structure of the Cdots. Figure S3 shows the Cdots 13C NMR, 1H NMR, gCOSY-NMR, gCHSQC and gHMBC data after SEC purification. For improved analysis, the most representative signals from 1H NMR, 13C NMR, gCOSY-NMR and gCHSQC were collected in Table 1 .

The 1D and 2D spectral information suggests a small molecule of 4 unsaturated carbons and with a carboxylic and/or aldehyde group[59]. The molecule or molecules present are most possibly butyric acid and/or butanal (Fig. 4 ). These structures can be interpreted in two different ways: the first, as the monomers that constitute the Cdots [61,62]; and the second, as the monomers that conform the surface structure of the Cdots [35]. In the first case, assuming that the monomers constitute the entire structure of the Cdots, Cdots of the PDs kind would be present, which are merely conformed of a polymeric structure. In the second case, Cdots of the PCDs kind would be present with a surface polymeric structure surrounding a small aromatic core.

Experimental results suggest the presence of PDs and/or PCDs. The synthesis method used supports the obtained results. The “bottom-up” method focuses on the reticulation reactions of molecules or polymers containing amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl, or other active groups, in which polymerization and carbonization play a fundamental role in the formation of particles[63].

The great majority of Cdots obtained by the “bottom-up” route should belong to the range of PDs in the strict sense[25] (Fig. 5
). Synthesis by the “bottom-up” method requires precursors to possess certain polymerizable elements, including dehydratable functional groups, reticulated sites of action, or unsaturated additive bonds. The proper selection of precursors with symmetrical factors must obtain PDs by the “bottom-up” method. Appropriate carbonization is conducive to promoting the conversion of PDs to Cdots[23,25]. Using microwaves and relatively gentle methods such as hydrothermal and solvothermal, and with short reaction times, little or no carbonization will probably be obtained[23,64]. Nevertheless, it is also an option that PCDs are present. These have a small carbonized core surrounded by polymers. Polymer structure that is conformed by the structures[35] described in Fig. 4.

The stability of PCDs is better than PDs due to carbonization, while the compatibility of PCDs is better than Cdots due to polymer chains[25]. To discriminate between PDs and PCDs, stability studies were performed by 1H NMR. Figure S4 shows the 1H NMR spectra of Cdots measured every week and during one month. No change in the spectra was observed, suggesting high stability. Therefore, Cdots in the study probably have a small carbonized core that gives it more stability and could not be identified by NMR, and a considerable polymer sphere surrounding it.

This hybrid structure combines the advantages of both kinds of Cdots. However, stability studies are not sufficient to determine if the kind of Cdots is PDs or PCDs. Whether it is one or the other, or a combination of both, the structural characterization results are good and could be very useful in the search for a potential application. Both PDs and PCDs have advantages over other kinds of Cdots. Both kinds of Cdots are environmentally friendly and low cost, non-toxic and metabolizable, with precursors widely available and easy to prepare[64]. They also have abundant surface groups, which provides them with sufficiently hydrophilic properties without the necessity of modifying them, thus favoring their application on living organisms[25].

Owing to the fact that the inference of the formation of core structure based only on experimental measurements is really difficult, we also performed a theoretical study in order to define if the detected molecules constitute PDs or PCDs structures. Regarding the formation of a core structure, we will focus the theoretical analysis in the UV–visible characteristics and its interpretation, which constitutes an effective tool to infer the constitution of Cdots by comparison of the experimental results and theoretical predictions. It is already known that a main peak at approximately 270 nm correspond to π-π* transitions[3,43]. This peak appears in the measured UV–visible spectra of the present work, consequently it is probable that a core structure exists, however we must be sure if the detected monomers or their polymerization cannot generate the same peak. For that reason, initially we performed a theoretical study based on the monomers obtained by NMR technique, which is included in the supplemental material. In the Figure S5 we present the molecules used to model the Cdot properties. Supported on this analysis we conclude that a contribution of this kind of structures to the UV–visible spectra behavior observed in Fig. 2 should be discarded.

Fig. 6 shows the experimental UV–visible spectra of the Cdots in which two absorption bands at 270 and 338 nm are identified as shown previously in Fig. 2. The bands at 270 and 338 nm correspond to the C = C and COO– groups, respectively[3,6,9]. The UV–visible bands provide important information for theoretical studies.

From the experimental results, it is clear that the monomers proposed can be polymerized forming PDs. However, this kind of structures, as was inferred in the theoretical analysis developed in the supplemental information, presents UV–visible spectra that do not match with the experimental one. A reason could be that, instead of PDs, during the synthesis small carbon core forms yielding to PCDs. For that reason, we proposed an aromatic structure to model the core and predict the UV–visible spectra for such case. For this calculation, two types of core basic units for building the core models were used; a coronene core and a small polyaromatic molecule as proposed in previous works [9,43]. Both models correspond to a fundamental fragment that could constitute the core and are shown in Figure S12 .

It is interesting to observe in Fig. 7 (b) that using B3LYP functional the absorption peak due to π-π* transition caused by polarization of the molecule along its length direction is at approximately 264 nm, which is in very good concordance with the experimental peak at 270 nm observed in Fig. 6. Instead, for coronene, this peak is approximately at 315 nm.

Based on this theoretical analysis, the assumption of a core structure explains very well the experimental UV–visible spectra in Fig. 6, and it is probably constituted by small PAH molecules like anthracene instead of larger ones like coronene, as was proposed in a previous work [43]. In addition, they provide a solid basis to affirm that the monomers or polymers detected by NMR constitute a shell and PCDs are formed. According to the theoretical prediction, only the core structure can be responsible of the spectroscopic characteristics measured in the UV–visible range. In a qualitative description, the radiation apparently is transmitted by the shell of monomer/polymers yielding to a plasmon resonance on the core surface.

A systematic theoretical study was performed by B. Zhi et. al. [30], who also investigate the thermodynamics of possible formation reactions to increase the number of molecules candidates. However, the absorption peaks are strong red shifted compared to the experimental spectra of this work. This is mainly because the molecules derive from different precursors, which highly influence the possible chromopheres that can be formed. Previously [66,67], the Cdots have also been studied experimentally and theoretically by nitrogen doped models and polymers with nitrogen groups, but are not adequate to model the Cdots of the present work, where glucose is used as precursor.

Finally, the Cdots IR spectra are shown in Fig. 9 . The region between 950 and 1800 cm−1 was analyzed, because chemical bonds of interest are found. The characteristic peaks of the vibrations C = C and C = O, between 1500 cm−1 and 1710 cm−1, are observed[3,9]. The peak around 1550 cm-1 is due to the presence of water in the sample. The C = O group that was identified in the NMR analysis is specifically at 1730 cm−1 [68]. The peaks between 1000 and 1400 cm−1 correspond to the stretching of C-OH and the flexion vibration of OH, due to the residual hydroxyl groups on the surface of the Cdots[3,9]. These groups are generated due to dehydration and decarboxylation of glucose[69]. It is consistent with NMR analysis and suggests the presence of carboxylic acid. Furthermore, the IR spectra also provide important information that relates with theoretical studies in this work.
A theoretical study of the IR spectra was performed. In Fig. 10 is observed the IR vibrational spectra of the butanoic acid and butanal monomers, and the coronene and antracene molecules.

Through an analysis of the IR active normal modes, it can be identified which type of vibration contributes to the different IR bands. In that sense, butanoic acid bands at 1067 and 1115 cm−1 corresponds to O-H bending, C-O stretching and also contributions of H wagging and bending and intermediate C stretching. These contributions remain at 1359 cm−1 but with also some C = O bending and C-O stretching. Then the band at 1755 cm−1 is mainly due to C = O stretching and O-H bending. Butanal also contributes with bands in the region 1200–1450 cm−1, where the higher intense peak at 1333 cm−1 corresponds to the H wagging and C stretching. Finally, the band at 1744 cm−1 it is due to C = O stretching. This absorption bands are in concordance with the absorption peaks observed in the experimental spectra (Fig. 9).

The same analysis was performed for coronene and anthracene structures. It shows that for coronene the bands at 1111 and 1301 cm−1 correspond to H bending, with also some contribution at the later normal mode of the C = C stretching of the C atoms in the center of the molecule. Furthermore, new IR bands that are not observed in monomers spectra are the bands at 1487 and 1604 cm−1, which correspond to normal modes related with C = C stretching mainly. Similarly, bands at 1002 and 1127 cm−1 correspond to H bending for anthracene, while bands at 1534 and 1618 cm−1 are related with C = C stretching. This bands were also observed in the experimental IR spectra (Fig. 9), in concordance with the formation of core that has structures with C = C conjugated bonds.

The comparison between experimental and theoretical UV–visible and IR spectra shows that it is required of both carbon core structures and the polymer core shell, to explain satisfactorily, the features observed in the experimental spectra. The present work constitutes a good base for future studies on this type of Cdots, including a more systematic approach as performed by B. Zhi et. al. [30] for other precursors, which can contribute to increase the conformational sampling to achieve a precise description of the system composition. Nevertheless, for this challenging task, a deep research is needed to explore reactions paths to obtain other possible monomers from glucose as precursor, which can be evaluated thermodynamically. Subsequently, explore the polymerization using molecular dynamics and other ensemble sampling methods like replica exchange molecular dynamics (REMD)[70].

The Cdots were synthesized using glucose as a precursor and via the microwave method (“bottom-up” route) as a quite simple and environmentally friendly method. Prior to the experimental characterization, the Cdots were purified by filtration, dialysis and column chromatography. The characterization was performed by UV–visible, Fluorescence, IR and NMR and theoretically by DFT and TDDFT calculations. The experimental results obtained by High-resolution solution 1D and 2D NMR suggest the presence of two monomers that constitutes the structure of the Cdots as a polymeric structure. A four-carbon carboxylic acid was elucidated and another monomer or molecule was identified with the presence of an aldehyde group in their structure. The analysis and interpretation of experimental results suggest the presence of PDs, PCDs or a combination of both. The calculations obtained by DFT and TDDFT clarified these results based on the theoretical predictions of the UV–visible and IR spectra behavior for the structures detected experimentally. The UV–visible theoretical results provide a solid base to infer that the monomers or polymers detected by NMR technique are unable to generate the peaks observed in the UV–visible spectra, being the shell of monomers or polymers transparent to the radiation in that region of the spectrum. According to these results, the main peak observed in UV–visible spectra is related with π-π* transitions of PAH molecules with not many aromatic rings. These types of molecules probably constitute the carbon core of the Cdots, and are detected by the UV–visible radiation transmitted through the polymers shell. However, theoretical IR calculation shows that both the proposed monomers and the core structure are fundamental to generate some of the bands observed experimentally. For that reason, the DFT and TDDFT calculations performed support the formation of PCDs. PCDs have advantages compared to other kinds of Cdots, these are environmentally friendly and low cost, non-toxic and metabolizable, with precursors that are widely available and easy to prepare. They have abundant surface groups thus favoring their application in different areas of science. The structural characterization is good and could be very useful in the development of a possible application. The methodology employed in this paper could be used to structurally characterize other kinds of Cdots with different types of synthesis and precursors, an aspect that is of crucial importance, both from fundamental and application points of view.

J.M. Arroyave: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. R.E. Ambrusi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Y. Robein: Investigation. M.E. Pronsato: Supervision, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. G. Brizuela: Supervision, Investigation. M.S. Di Nezio: Supervision, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. M.E. Centurión: Supervision, Investigation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support from SGCyT-UNS (PGI–UNS 24/Q097 and PGI-UNS 24/F072) and ANPCyT (PICT-2016-4094). Ambrusi, R. E., Robein, Y., Pronsato, M. E., and Brizuela, G. are members of CONICET and Arroyave, J. M. is a Postdoctoral fellow of this institution. We also thank the University of Trieste (Italy) for its kind hospitality during the NMR data collection.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Density functional theory; a practical introduction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sholl DS, Steckel JA, Density functional theory; a practical introduction, John Wiley & sons, Inc., Canada, 2009.
